findings.description: _is_expired has its comparison inverted (returns age < self.ttl, i.e. True for FRESH entries), so get() evicts every fresh L1 entry and serves entries older than the TTL as hits indefinitely; should be age >= self.ttl.

pickle.loads on bytes read from Redis is unsafe deserialization (CWE-502): anyone who can write to the shared Redis store gains arbitrary code execution in every process that reads the cache; a safe format (JSON) or signed payloads should be used.

get_or_compute cannot cache falsy-None results: a stored None is returned by get() (which even counts it as a hit) but the 'is not None' check treats it as a miss, so compute() and set() re-run on every call for legitimately-None values.

RedisClient.setex silently ignores its ttl_seconds parameter, so L2 entries never expire and any value evicted from L1 is resurrected from Redis forever (acknowledged fixture stub, but it violates the setex contract the cache relies on).
_local is unbounded with only lazy per-key eviction on get(); there is no size cap, LRU, or periodic sweep, so a long-running process touching many distinct keys grows memory without limit.

Not thread-safe: the check-then-act sequence (entry lookup then 'del self._local[key]') can raise KeyError if a concurrent get()/invalidate() removes the key in between, and the unsynchronized stats increments can lose updates.

stats.local_entries is only refreshed inside set(), so it goes stale after get()-path evictions/promotions and invalidate(); export_stats() bypasses it with len(self._local), leaving the dataclass field permanently inconsistent.
Promoting an L2 hit into L1 stamps a fresh time.time(), restarting the TTL clock so a value can be served up to roughly twice its intended TTL (and indefinitely given the non-expiring stub L2).

TTL ages are computed from wall-clock time.time(), which can step backward/forward under NTP adjustment causing premature expiry or indefinite retention; time.monotonic() is the correct basis for elapsed-time checks.
invalidate() pops L1 before deleting from Redis, so a concurrent get() can read the old value from Redis and re-promote it into L1 after invalidation completes, resurrecting the invalidated entry.
get_or_compute has no stampede protection (lock/single-flight), so all concurrent callers that miss simultaneously execute compute() redundantly for the same key.
set() writes L1 before L2 with no rollback: if redis.setex raises, this process keeps serving a value that no other process can ever see, silently diverging the tiers.
ttl_seconds is not validated; zero or negative values are accepted and produce degenerate expiry behavior instead of an early ValueError.
The module-level logger (and the logging import) is never used anywhere in the file; either log cache events (e.g. deserialization failures) or drop the dead setup.
Style mixes typing.Optional/Callable with PEP 585 builtin generics like dict[str, bytes]; the implied Python version supports the consistent 'X | None' and collections.abc.Callable forms.
